title: Approaches & Research Strategies
simple_html:
Once you know your philosophy (Lesson 2), the next two onion layers ask: how will you use theory? (your approach) and what overall plan will you follow? (your strategy). Getting these right keeps your study logical from start to finish.

Approaches to theory

  • Deductive — start with an existing theory, turn it into a testable hypothesis, then collect data to confirm or reject it. (Theory → data.)
  • Inductive — start by collecting data, look for patterns, and build a new theory from them. (Data → theory.)
  • Abductive — go back and forth between data and theory to find the best explanation. (A practical mix.)

Research strategies

A strategy is the overall game plan for answering your question:

  • Experiment — manipulate a variable to test cause and effect.
  • Survey — ask many people standardised questions.
  • Case study — study one or a few cases in rich depth.
  • Action research — solve a real problem while studying it.
  • Grounded theory — build theory directly from data.
  • Ethnography — immerse in a group to understand its culture.
  • Archival research — analyse existing records and documents.

Key takeaways

  • Approach = how theory is used (deductive, inductive, abductive).
  • Strategy = the overall plan (survey, experiment, case study, etc.).
  • Deductive + survey/experiment fits positivism; inductive + case study/ethnography fits interpretivism.
  • Choose the strategy that best answers your specific question.
intermediate_html:
The approach and strategy layers of the research onion translate your philosophy into a concrete plan. The approach decides how theory enters the study; the strategy decides the overall method by which the research question will be answered. They are distinct decisions that must nonetheless cohere with each other and with the philosophy above them.

Approaches to theory development

  • Deductive — the dominant logic of the natural sciences. You begin with theory, derive one or more falsifiable hypotheses, operationalise the variables, collect data, and test. It seeks to explain causal relationships and generalise. Pairs with positivism and quantitative methods.
  • Inductive — you begin with observations, identify patterns and themes, and construct theory grounded in the data. It seeks understanding of meanings in context. Pairs with interpretivism and qualitative methods.
  • Abductive — you start from a surprising fact, propose the most plausible explanation, then iterate between data and theory to refine it. Pairs with critical realism and pragmatism, and underpins much mixed-methods work.

The main strategies

StrategyBest forTypical approach
ExperimentCause-and-effect (does X change Y?)Deductive, quantitative
SurveyWho/what/how many/how much; relationshipsDeductive, quantitative
Case studyHow/why in real-life context, in depthInductive or abductive
Action researchSolving a practical problem with stakeholdersIterative, participatory
Grounded theoryBuilding theory where little existsInductive
EthnographyUnderstanding a culture or group from withinInductive, qualitative
Archival/documentaryQuestions answerable from existing recordsEither

Worked example

A retailer wants to know whether a new store layout boosts sales. The question is causal and measurable, so the natural design is deductive with an experiment strategy: redesign a sample of stores (treatment), leave matched stores unchanged (control), and compare sales over three months. Now change the question to "How do shoppers experience the new layout?" The verb experience flips the design to inductive with a case study or ethnography: observe and interview shoppers in a few stores, then build themes about navigation, comfort, and discovery. Same retailer, different question, opposite ends of the approach/strategy space — each internally consistent.

How to apply it

  1. Match approach to philosophy. Positivist → deductive; interpretivist → inductive; critical realist/pragmatist → abductive.
  2. Match strategy to the question type. "Does X cause Y?" → experiment. "How many / what relationship?" → survey. "How / why, in depth?" → case study. "Build new theory?" → grounded theory.
  3. Check feasibility: access, time, ethics, and your own skills constrain the strategy as much as logic does.
  4. Justify in writing: state why your chosen strategy answers the question better than the alternatives you rejected.

This layer sits between Research Philosophy (Lesson 2) and the practical design decisions of Research Design & the Literature Review (Lesson 4); strategies such as the experiment also drive the sampling (Lesson 5) and analysis (Lesson 8) choices downstream.
full_html:
Having declared a philosophy (Lesson 2), the researcher confronts the next two layers of the research onion: the approach to theory development and the research strategy. These layers convert abstract belief into a workable plan, and their coherence with each other — and with the philosophy above — is what examiners scrutinise most closely (Saunders, Lewis & Thornhill, 2019).

The logic of approaches

Deductive reasoning embodies the hypothetico-deductive model associated with Popper's (1959) principle of falsifiability: a general theory yields specific, testable hypotheses; data are gathered to test them; and a single robust disconfirmation can falsify the theory, while corroboration merely fails to refute it. Deduction prizes structure, replication, and generalisation, and is tightly bound to positivism and quantitative measurement. Inductive reasoning reverses the flow, moving from specific observations to general theory. It is vulnerable to Hume's classic problem of induction — no finite set of observations logically guarantees a universal claim — but it excels at generating rich, context-sensitive theory, and aligns with interpretivism. Abductive reasoning, traced to Peirce, begins with a surprising or anomalous observation and infers the most plausible explanation, then iterates between empirical material and conceptual development. Abduction underpins critical realist and pragmatist designs and most genuine mixed-methods studies (Bryman & Bell, 2015), and it more faithfully describes how experienced researchers actually work, dialectically rather than in a single direction.

The strategies

Saunders et al. (2019) catalogue the principal strategies, each suited to particular questions:

  • Experiment — the gold standard for causal inference. Manipulate an independent variable, control for confounds (ideally via randomisation, after Fisher's design-of-experiments tradition), and measure the effect on a dependent variable. True experiments require random assignment; quasi-experiments approximate this when randomisation is impractical (Campbell & Stanley, 1963).
  • Survey — collects standardised data from a (usually large) sample to describe populations and test associations. Economical and generalisable, but limited in establishing causation and dependent on instrument quality.
  • Case study — an in-depth, empirical inquiry into a phenomenon within its real-life context, especially where the boundary between phenomenon and context is unclear (Yin, 2018). Strong on "how" and "why" questions; uses multiple sources and analytic (not statistical) generalisation.
  • Action research — a participatory, cyclical strategy (plan–act–observe–reflect) that simultaneously solves a practical problem and generates knowledge (Lewin, 1946); the researcher is an agent of change, not a detached observer.
  • Grounded theory — a systematic inductive method for building theory directly from data through constant comparison and theoretical sampling (Glaser & Strauss, 1967), later split into Glaserian, Straussian, and constructivist (Charmaz, 2006) variants.
  • Ethnography — immersive study of a group's culture in its natural setting through prolonged participant observation, rooted in anthropology.
  • Archival and documentary research — analysis of existing administrative records, texts, and secondary data, increasingly important in the era of "big data."

Worked example

A bank asks whether a financial-literacy programme reduces customer loan defaults. Because the question is causal and the outcome is measurable, the coherent chain is: positivism → deductive → quantitative → experiment. Randomly assign eligible customers to receive the programme or not, then compare 12-month default rates using a difference-in-differences model. Suppose, instead, the bank asks how front-line staff make sense of customers' financial anxieties. The chain becomes interpretivism → inductive → qualitative → ethnography or case study: shadow and interview staff across several branches and build themes. A pragmatic version — abductive, mixed methods — would run the experiment and interview participants to explain why the programme worked or failed, integrating the two strands (Tashakkori & Teddlie, 1998).

Nuances and debates

First, the neat pairing of "deductive = quantitative, inductive = qualitative" is a useful heuristic but not a law: qualitative data can test propositions deductively, and quantitative data can be explored inductively. Second, experiments dominate discussions of causality, yet field experiments raise external-validity and ethical concerns, and many important business questions cannot be ethically or practically randomised — hence the rise of quasi-experimental and natural-experiment designs. Third, case study suffers a persistent misconception that it cannot generalise; Yin (2018) and Flyvbjerg (2006) counter that it generalises analytically to theory, not statistically to populations, and that the "single case is worthless" view is itself a fallacy. Fourth, grounded theory is frequently invoked loosely as a synonym for "qualitative coding," ignoring its demanding requirements of theoretical sampling and constant comparison.

Common mistakes

  • Choosing a strategy by familiarity ("I'll run a survey") rather than by question type.
  • Claiming causation from a cross-sectional survey, which can establish association but rarely causation.
  • Labelling any in-depth qualitative project a "case study" without defining the case, its boundaries, or its unit of analysis.
  • Mixing an inductive strategy with hypothesis testing language, or vice versa — a sign of misalignment.
  • Treating action research as ordinary consultancy, omitting the reflective, knowledge-generating cycle that defines it.

Cross-links

Approach and strategy descend directly from Research Philosophy (Lesson 2) and the overarching logic of the research onion (Lesson 1). They set up the practical decisions of Research Design & the Literature Review (Lesson 4), determine the sampling logic in Sampling (Lesson 5), shape the instruments in Data Collection & Measurement (Lesson 6), and dictate whether you will speak the language of reliability/validity or trustworthiness (Lesson 7) and run statistical or thematic analysis (Lesson 8).
